one_line_takeaway: A PRISMA-style meta-review of thirteen malicious-code-refusal prompt corpora finds a uniform, three-year-spanning absence of inter-rater-reliability reporting, access gating, and takedown policy across the field, and catalogues (but does not re-measure) each corpus's own construction methodology, refusal-rate figures, and malware-category coverage.
key_finding: The review's headline empirical observation (an audit finding, not a new measurement) is that zero of the thirteen surveyed corpora report a Fleiss'-kappa-style inter-rater reliability statistic on their released refusal labels -- visualized in Figure 5's Reproducibility and Reliability Gap Matrix, where the IRR column is uniformly red across all thirteen rows. Two companion audit findings: zero of thirteen corpora gate access at first release (all ship fully open, even corpora containing executable malware payloads or jailbreak templates with documented success rates above 60%), and zero document a takedown/responsible-disclosure policy. The review also surfaces that no canonical malware-category taxonomy spans the thirteen corpora (schemas range from MOCHA's 13 functional families to CySecBench's 10 attack types to no formal taxonomy at all in several corpora), and that refusal-rate statistics reported across corpora are not directly comparable because they measure non-equivalent constructs (differing prompt composition, elicitation modality, and labeling protocol). All quantitative per-corpus figures reported in the review (e.g., RMCBench's 28.71% aggregate refusal rate, 40.36% text-to-code vs 11.52% code-to-code; MalwareBench's 60.93% seed rejection falling to 39.92% post-jailbreak; MOCHA's 13.0-54.5% closed-model and 2.5-49.0% open-model rejection rates with gaps up to -54.1 points; CySecBench's reported 65.4% ChatGPT / 88.4% Gemini / 17.4% Claude prompt-obfuscation attack success rates; JAWS-Bench's ~71% (JAWS-1) and ~75% (JAWS-M) mean attack-success rates with a ~1.6x agentic amplification factor) are the authors' own catalogued figures pulled verbatim from each cited corpus's original publication, not new measurements by this paper.
core_contribution: This is a PRISMA-style systematic review, not a new benchmark or empirical study. The authors (Young and Moody, UNLV) treat malicious-code-generation prompt corpora themselves as the unit of analysis for the first time, applying a uniform extraction template to thirteen in-scope corpora released 2023-2025 (AdvBench, the CyberSecEval family v1-v3, RMCBench, RedCode, MCGMark/MCGTest, JailbreakBench, CySecBench, MalwareBench, CIRCLE, MOCHA, ASTRA, Scam2Prompt/Innoc2Scam-bench, and JAWS-Bench). The paper's own four synthesis artefacts are: (1) a cross-corpus methodology comparison table extracting each corpus along ten axes (construction method, prompt count, modality, taxonomy, target-model panel, original IRR statistic, license, venue); (2) a prompt-construction taxonomy formalizing modality, turn structure, and elicitation style; (3) a reproducibility/licensing landscape table; and (4) a 22-category union malware-coverage map (Figure 4) built by consolidating the thirteen corpora's own published or author-authored per-category prompt counts. The paper explicitly states its contribution 'is synthesis, not new measurement': no original refusal-rate experiments, judge panels, or model evaluations are run by the authors in this paper (a companion, separately-published prompt-bank paper [21] and an unpublished thirteen-model behavioural baseline supply methodological substrate but are explicitly disclosed as authored by the same team and out of scope here).
threat_model: Not applicable in the conventional sense -- this is a meta-level review of evaluation methodology, not an attack or defense paper. The object under study is the threat-model boundary used by others: the paper defines its inclusion scope as corpora that measure whether a coding LLM or code-interpreter agent refuses to generate executable malicious software in response to explicit or jailbreak-decorated requests (as opposed to corpora that measure whether benign-request code output is inadvertently vulnerable, which the paper explicitly excludes as a different construct). The paper draws a refusal-vs-vulnerability distinction and a content-safety-vs-code-safety distinction as its own conceptual contributions used to scope inclusion/exclusion.
limitations: Limitations the paper itself states: (1) the review performs descriptive cataloging of already-published metadata, explicitly distinguished from the interpretive refusal-labeling task it critiques in surveyed corpora, and the authors acknowledge their own extraction process is 'subject to error' with 'no human-baseline calibration' of that process; (2) it does not pool refusal-rate statistics or re-measure inter-rater reliability under a uniform protocol, because the corpora are judged too heterogeneous -- a uniform re-evaluation using the companion five-judge panel is explicitly deferred to future work (Section 9.5); (3) the 22-category coverage map (Figure 4) is qualitative, built from a mix of each corpus's published per-category breakdown and a documented uniform-allocation assumption where no breakdown exists (RedCode, AdvBench, JBB-Cyberweapons, and CIRCLE do not publish per-category counts), and is explicitly flagged as 'not a re-measurement'; (4) five of the thirteen surveyed corpora (RMCBench, MCGTest, MalwareBench, Scam2Prompt, JAWS-Bench) ship without an explicit stated license, and several fields (e.g., per-release CyberSecEval prompt counts) are marked 'n/r' because they could not be resolved from primary sources; (5) the authors disclose overlapping authorship with a companion prompt-bank paper [21] cited as a methodological exemplar throughout, and flag this explicitly wherever cited to avoid conflating Related Work with novel contribution here.

datasets_used: CyberSecEval family v1-v3 (1,000 prompts in v1; v2/v3 raw counts n/r)
RedCode (4,050 RedCode-Exec test cases + 160 RedCode-Gen prompts)
MalwareBench (3,520 jailbreaking prompts from 320 manual seeds)
MOCHA (1,821 single-turn + 5,430 jailbroken + 3,601 multi-turn = 10,852 total, plus 10,084/200/200 train/val/test split)
quantitative_results: RMCBench: 28.71% aggregate refusal rate (40.36% text-to-code, 11.52% code-to-code) across 11 LLMs

MalwareBench: 60.93% average content-rejection on seed requirements, falling to 39.92% under jailbreak transformation, across 29 LLMs; GPT-4o judge agreement ~80.3% with 300 expert-annotated responses

MOCHA: rejection rates 13.0-54.5% (closed models) and 2.5-49.0% (open models) across 20 models; multi-turn attacks open gaps up to -54.1 points (Qwen2.5-Coder-14B); LoRA fine-tuning on MOCHA-train improves rejection up to 36% in-distribution / 32.4% on external benchmarks
CySecBench: prompt-obfuscation attack success 65.4% (ChatGPT), 88.4% (Gemini), 17.4% (Claude); 78.5% transfer success rate on AdvBench

JAWS-Bench: mean attack-success ~71% (JAWS-1) and ~75% (JAWS-M), 32% of JAWS-M outputs instantly deployable; JAWS-0 prompt-only regime 61% acceptance, 27% run end-to-end; agent harness raises ASR ~1.6x vs plain-prompt
CyberSecEval 2: prompt-injection success 26-41% (compliance rate 59-74%) across GPT-4/Mistral/Llama-3-70B/Code Llama
CIRCLE: order-of-magnitude refusal disparity within one provider, e.g. OpenAI o4-mini refuses 7.1% of risky requests vs GPT-4.1 at 0.5%

ASTRA: attack-success rates above 70% on software-security guidance and above 50% on secure-code generation against hardened production systems; claims 11-66% more issues discovered vs prior techniques and 17% more effective alignment training when fed back as fine-tuning data
Scam2Prompt: malicious-code-generation rates 12.7-43.8% across an 11-model panel
Zero of thirteen corpora report a Fleiss'-kappa-style inter-rater reliability statistic on released refusal labels (this review's own audit finding)
Zero of thirteen corpora gate access at release; zero document a takedown policy (this review's own audit findings)
baselines_compared: Not applicable in the conventional model-vs-baseline sense: this is a review paper. The 'comparisons' are cross-corpus methodology comparisons (Table 1: construction method, modality, taxonomy, target-panel, IRR, license, venue) and a reproducibility/licensing comparison (Table 2), assembled from each surveyed corpus's own reported baselines and target-model panels rather than any baseline run by this paper's authors.
